title: Purple Spots or Bruising on a Cat's Belly: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat has purple spots or bruising on their belly, it may be a medical emergency. Learn about petechiae, ecchymosis, and when to seek care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Purple spots on a cat's belly indicate internal bleeding and represent a high-priority veterinary emergency. Contact an emergency vet immediately and take a clear photo of the area to help track the progression of the bruising.

What do purple or dark red spots on my cat's belly mean?

Purple or dark red spots on a cat's skin, particularly in areas with less fur like the abdomen, are usually signs of internal bleeding under the skin. Smaller, pin-prick spots are called petechiae, while larger, bruise-like patches are known as ecchymosis. These marks indicate that blood is leaking from the vessels, which can be caused by clotting disorders, immune-mediated diseases, or exposure to toxins.

Are purple spots on my cat's skin a veterinary emergency?

  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately, as unexplained bruising is a high-urgency condition that often points to life-threatening systemic problems.
  • Watch for additional warning signs such as lethargy, pale gums, or rapid breathing.
  • Understand that these spots can indicate severe issues like dangerously low platelet counts or ingestion of anticoagulant rodenticides (rat poison).

How does taking a photo of the bruising help with my cat's triage?

  • Provide your veterinary team with a clear, well-lit photograph to help them determine if the bruising is spreading or worsening during transit.
  • Use natural light and gently move the fur aside to ensure the clearest possible view of the affected area.
  • Use the photo as a tool to show the doctor the exact state of the skin at a specific point in time.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Retroperitoneal, fascial, or intramuscular (body wall) hemorrhage can be challenging to identify. Hemorrhage into the GI system may also present similarly. Abdominocentesis revealing fluid with a PCV >5% indicates significant abdominal hemorrhage that warrants careful monitoring. Cytologic examination of abdominal fluid should be performed to evaluate for WBCs, plant or meat fibers, and free or intracellular bacteria. Emergency exploratory laparotomy is indicated with ongoing hemorrhage or the inability to stabilize shock.

Chapter: Hepatology, Emergency, Internal Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 473)

faqs.question: Can I wait a few days to see if the bruises fade?
faqs.answer: No. Because purple spots often indicate a failure in the blood's ability to clot, waiting can lead to fatal internal bleeding. Immediate evaluation is necessary.

faqs.question: What could cause sudden bruising in a cat?
faqs.answer: Common causes include immune-mediated thrombocytopenia (IMT), ingestion of rat poison, severe infections, or organ failure.

faqs.question: Will my cat need blood tests?
faqs.answer: Yes, your veterinarian will likely perform a Complete Blood Count (CBC) and a coagulation profile to check platelet levels and how well the blood is clotting.
